paragraphs.text: Movingly Judah spoke to Joseph, until Joseph was overcome by love for his brothers; he not only restored Benjamin to them, as they had hoped, but revealed himself to them, which they had not expected. Joseph ordered all to be sent away from him, for over a feigned crime he had judged them in front of all; intending to judge them in an actual crime, he wished to remove all the witnesses. When all in wonder had withdrawn, then he changed his language and voice, and now without an interpreter, in the Hebrew tongue, he said: “I am Joseph your brother. And his brothers could not answer him; for they were troubled” (Gen 45:3). They feared that upon the disclosure of the crime he would put them to death. And Joseph feared that if he should say to his brothers: “I am the one whom you sold as a slave,” then the Egyptians standing outside the door would hear this and despise his brothers. Therefore he says to them: “Come near to me.” And when they had come near, softly he continues his speech: “I am Joseph… whom ye sold into Egypt” (Gen 45:4). And seeing that they were sad and from shame could not look at him, he comforts them with these words: “Now therefore be not grieved… that ye sold me hither: for God did send me before you to preserve life… for yet there are five years of famine, in which there shall neither be earing nor harvest” (Gen 45:5–6). And that my prediction is true is sufficiently shown by the seven good years that have passed. “Haste ye, and go up to my father, and say unto him: …God hath made me lord” not only of my brothers, as the dreams had foretold me, “but of all the land of Egypt,” which the dreams had not promised (Gen 45:9). And tell him of “all my glory in Egypt” (Gen 45:13), that he may give thanks to God for all that has happened to me in Egypt.

After this Joseph kissed Benjamin, and they wept upon each other’s neck; Joseph also kissed his other brothers. When Joseph’s brothers were assured that Joseph bore no anger against them, then they opened their mouths and began to converse with him.

And when their conversation behind the closed doors of the house was finished, then with happy faces the nobles and commanders entered: “and it pleased Pharaoh well, and his servants” (Gen 45:16). For they now learned for certain that it was not a slave who had become as a father to Pharaoh and lord of all the freemen and nobles in Egypt, but the son of free men from a blessed race, from the house of Abraham.

Joseph, having given his brothers clothes, chariots, and all manner of Egyptian goods, sent them to bring his father into Egypt. “And he said to them: See that ye fall not out by the way” (Gen 45:24). By this Joseph forbade them to reproach one another and as it were to say: “Thou advised to cast Joseph into the pit,” or, “No, thou insisted that Joseph be sold, bound and naked, to the Arabs.” “As I,” Joseph meant, “have forgiven all of you, so do you all forgive one another, that from quarrels and reproaches our present joy be not turned for you into sorrow.”

Joseph’s brothers set out on their way, rejoicing that they had found Joseph, and at the same time troubled how to justify themselves before their father. And so they come to Jacob, recount everything to him. And he, seeing the chariots and gifts, willingly believes them. And “his spirit revived” (Gen 45:27) and he says: “It is great — for all of us, but yet more for me — Joseph my son is yet alive” (Gen 45:28). When they had told Jacob of Joseph’s glory, of his wisdom in governing, of Joseph’s final judgment over the brothers, which had been more bitter for them than the first, then their father said to them: “Why did ye not ask Joseph, how and why did he go down to Egypt?” All of them, looking at one another, did not know what to answer. Only Judah opened his mouth and said to his father: “We confess now our transgression. From Joseph’s dreams the brothers, in their simplicity, concluded that thou and we should be in bondage to Joseph. And in their folly they reasoned that it was better for him alone to be a slave than for us and our father to be in bondage. So the brothers did this in pity for thee and Benjamin, not because thou didst love Joseph. For thou lovest also Benjamin, but since he does not say that we shall be in bondage to him, we all love him too. Therefore forgive us, that we humiliated Joseph, for this humiliation has raised him to the heights of greatness.” The father accepted the justification and said: “For the news about Joseph with which you have gladdened me, your transgression is forgiven, the report of which had caused me sorrow.”
